forests.foodLimit: Huckleberries and personal-use berries may be picked anywhere on the Idaho Panhandle National Forests, which include Coeur d'Alene, unless a spot is specifically closed, with no permit. Commercial gathering is prohibited and damaging or removing bushes is illegal. Pick only what you can use. Other edible plants are not separately addressed.

forests.foodLimit: The National Forests and Grasslands in Texas publish no confirmable personal-use rule for gathering berries, fruit, or nuts here. forests.foodLimit: Holly Springs National Forest publishes no confirmable personal-use rule for gathering edible fruits, nuts, or berries. forests.mushNote: No confirmable wild-mushroom rule is published for Holly Springs National Forest. The National Forests in Mississippi permits page does not address mushroom gathering. forests.foodLimit: Helena-Lewis and Clark National Forest, which now administers Lewis and Clark, publishes no confirmable personal-use rule for berries, fruits, or nuts. Contact the ranger district before gathering edible plants. Occurrence on the map is not permission to harvest.

forests.foodLimit: No permit required for gathering edible material for one's own consumption (personal use). forests.foodLimit: No permit needed for ordinary personal-use gathering of berries, nuts, and other listed botanical products. forests.mushNote: Fungi (including mushrooms) are explicitly named in the list of forest botanical products that "many can be gathered without the need for permits." forests.foodLimit: The Manti-La Sal states that a valid permit is required before collecting any forest product from its lands, and it publishes no free personal-use allowance for edible plants, berries, or nuts. Personal-use only; collecting must be at least 100 ft from any road, trail, campground, or facility.

forests.foodLimit: No permit needed for "a reasonable amount of edible material ... for your own consumption" (personal use). forests.mushNote: Mushrooms are explicitly named in the "When DON'T You Need a Pass or Permit" list — gathering a reasonable amount for personal consumption needs no permit. No separate free-use mushroom permit is required for personal use (unlike many forests).

forests.states: Oregon (and a small portion of northern California)

forests.mushNote: Mushrooms require a free personal-use permit. Up to 20 gallons per season and 5 gallons per day, cut in half lengthwise at collection. No gathering in Wild and Scenic River corridors.

forests.foodLimit: Personal use ("for your own consumption") needs no permit. forests.foodLimit: Homochitto National Forest publishes no confirmable personal-use rule for gathering edible fruits, nuts, or berries. forests.mushNote: Personal-use mushroom picking is free up to 5 gallons per day, but you must carry the free Incidental Use Mushroom Information Sheet while collecting. Mushrooms are for personal consumption only and cannot be sold.

forests.foodLimit: Ouachita National Forest publishes no confirmable personal-use rule for edible plants, berries, fruits, or nuts. Its permits page covers only commercial and special uses. Ask the local ranger district before harvesting. Occurrence on this map is not permission.

forests.foodLimit: The Carson lets you gather small amounts of herbs, cones, and conifer boughs for personal use without a permit, but berries, fruits, and nuts are not on that free list and no rule for them is published. Contact a Carson ranger district before gathering edible plants. Occurrence is not permission.
forests.mushNote: The Carson publishes no confirmable mushroom rule. Its free personal-use list names only herbs, cones, and boughs, and mushrooms are not included. Treat mushroom collecting as unverified, contact a Carson ranger district first, and remember occurrence on the map is not permission.

forests.states: New Mexico (also small areas in Arizona, Texas, Oklahoma via associated national grasslands)
forests.foodLimit: Cibola's forest products page states "Permits are required for gathering forest products," and the Southwestern Region (R03) permits page it links to lists piñon (pine) nuts among the forest products requiring a permit (alongside firewood, latillas/poles, and rock/minerals).
forests.mushNote: Cibola does not publish a mushroom-specific personal-use policy or a no-permit gallon/day threshold. Under its general rule that "permits are required for gathering forest products," wild mushroom collection is treated as permit-required (no separate free-use exemption is posted).

forests.foodLimit: No permit required for recreational berry picking, up to 10 gallons (personal/recreational use).
forests.mushNote: No permit required for incidental-use harvesting up to five gallons per season (approximately 2 grocery bags). Best practices required: cut/break mushrooms at ground level, no raking/digging/ground disturbance. Above the incidental personal-use threshold a permit would be needed.

forests.foodLimit: Helena-Lewis and Clark National Forest, which now administers Helena, publishes no confirmable personal-use rule for berries, fruits, or nuts. Contact the ranger district before gathering edible plants. Occurrence on the map is not permission to harvest.
forests.mushNote: Mushrooms follow the Northern Region system. Up to one gallon in possession, five gallons per season, needs no permit. Beyond that a free personal-use permit allows five gallons per day and twenty per season. Resale is prohibited.

forests.foodLimit: Kaibab National Forest publishes no confirmable personal-use rule for edible plants, berries, or nuts. Its permit program covers firewood, cinders, Christmas trees, commercial use, and tribal traditional use. Treat foraging as unverified and contact a Kaibab ranger district. Occurrence on this map is not harvest permission.
forests.mushNote: No confirmable personal-use mushroom rule is published for the general public. Treat mushroom gathering as unverified and contact a ranger district before harvesting. Occurrence on this map is not harvest permission.

forests.foodLimit: No incidental free-personal-use exemption is documented for edible fruits/berries/nuts. forests.mushNote: A permit is required for personal-use mushroom collection, but it is FREE. Per the forest's own page: "The forest issues free personal use and commercial mushroom permits." forests.foodLimit: The Ochoco does not address personal-use gathering of fruits, nuts, or berries. Treat plant foraging as unverified and contact a ranger district. Occurrence on this map is not harvest permission.
forests.mushNote: Personal mushroom picking needs a free-use permit from each Ranger District, up to two gallons per day for ten days, and every mushroom must be cut in half lengthwise. No collecting in wilderness or research natural areas, and no selling.

forests.foodLimit: Personal-use gathering of "small amounts" of berries, acorns, and black walnuts requires a permit from the nearest District Office; the permit "may include a minimal fee or even at no cost if forest products are for limited personal use." A permit is required anytime more than small amounts are gathered for personal us
forests.mushNote: Mushrooms are grouped with berries/acorns/nuts in the same guidance: a permit (minimal fee or no cost for limited personal use) is required from the District Office to gather small amounts of mushrooms; commercial gathering always requires a permit. No separate mushroom-specific threshold or whitelist is given.

forests.foodLimit: No permit needed for edible material gathered for one's own consumption; "edible material (morels, etc.)" is the forest's example. No explicit fruit/berry/nut quantity threshold stated on the page. A commercial/special-use permit is required to sell or for non-personal collection.
forests.mushNote: Mushrooms are explicitly covered by the personal-consumption exemption — the forest's own example of edible material with no permit is "morels, etc." So personal-use mushroom collection is free with no permit. (Commercial harvest still requires a permit.)

forests.foodLimit: Fruits/berries and nuts: free use, no permit required, no quantity limit, no commercial use (personal use only).
forests.mushNote: Mushrooms/fungi are free use with no permit required for personal use, but limited to 3 lbs or 1 gallon per person, per day; no commercial use. (Larger/commercial quantities require a permit from the local Ranger District.)

forests.foodLimit: Plumas's own permits page does not publish a personal-use free-use threshold for edible fruits/berries/nuts. Its blanket statement lists "edible material" among forest products that require a permit; no quantity threshold or personal-vs-commercial split is given on the forest's own page. Permits are obtained at Plumas
forests.mushNote: No Plumas-specific mushroom personal-use rule was found on the forest's own pages; mushrooms fall under the same blanket "edible material requires a permit" statement. forests.foodLimit: The Sabine publishes no confirmable personal-use gathering rule for edible plants. The Texas national forests permits page covers firewood only. An earlier nuts and berries allowance came from a wilderness page that no longer exists. Contact the Hemphill ranger district before harvesting. Occurrence on the map is not permission.
forests.mushNote: No mushroom rule is published for the Sabine or the Texas national forests. The permits page names firewood only and does not address fungi. Treat mushroom collecting as unverified, contact the Hemphill ranger district first, and remember occurrence on the map is not permission.

forests.foodLimit: The Salmon-Challis, which administers the former Salmon National Forest, publishes no confirmable berry, fruit, or edible-plant gathering rule. Its non-wood forest products page addresses mushrooms only. Contact a Salmon-Challis ranger district before harvesting edible plants. Occurrence on the map is not permission.
forests.mushNote: A permit is required to collect mushrooms on the Salmon-Challis for anyone age 12 and up. Incidental amounts under a gallon are free. A free personal-use permit covers up to 5 gallons per day and 20 per season. No resale.

forests.foodLimit: Free incidental personal use, no permit. Berries up to 1 gallon per day and 5 gallons per year. Fruits and seeds up to 1 pound per day and 20 pounds per year. No selling, none within 300 feet of water, campgrounds, or buildings, and none in wilderness.
forests.mushNote: Mushrooms may be gathered for personal use with no permit, up to 1 gallon per day and 5 gallons per year. Matsutake are excluded from free incidental use. No selling, and none within 300 feet of water, campgrounds, or buildings.

forests.foodLimit: The forest's permits page states that collecting and removing forest products — including edible material — requires a permit; no free incidental personal-use allowance for fruits/berries/nuts is stated. Permits are personal-use only (no commercial permits issued); must be carried

forests.foodLimit: Managed jointly with Francis Marion. No published free-use allowance for edible plants, berries, or nuts. Permits for miscellaneous forest products are sold at ranger district offices, with rules varying by district. Contact your district office before harvesting anything. Occurrence on this map is not permission.
forests.mushNote: Mushrooms are treated as a miscellaneous forest product needing a permit, sold at ranger district offices. No free personal-use allowance is published. Contact your district office before collecting. Occurrence on the map is not permission.

forests.foodLimit: The Umpqua publishes no confirmable berry or edible-plant gathering rule. Its special forest products page addresses mushrooms only. Contact an Umpqua ranger district before harvesting edible plants. Occurrence on the map is not permission.
forests.mushNote: Non-matsutake mushrooms under 1 gallon per person per day are free for incidental use, no permit, and never sold. Larger amounts need a free-use permit up to 2 gallons per day. Matsutake always require a paid permit, with a season opening after Labor Day.

forests.foodLimit: Incidental free personal use of "minor amounts" requires no permit; for planned/larger collection a permit may be obtained ("Permits may be obtained for collection of special forest products such as: cones, boughs, Christmas trees, nuts and berries..."). No published numeric per-day/per-year threshold for berries/fruit
forests.mushNote: Mushrooms are managed under a shared free-use/commercial permit program across the Deschutes, Fremont-Winema, Umpqua, and Willamette National Forests. forests.foodLimit: The Boise publishes no confirmable berry, fruit, or nut gathering rule. Its non-wood forest products page addresses mushrooms only. Contact a Boise ranger district before collecting edible plants. Occurrence on the map is not permission.
forests.mushNote: Mushrooms may be gathered for personal use with no permit, up to 5 gallons per person per day, with a possession limit of 10 gallons. Not for sale.

forests.foodLimit: No forest-wide free-use threshold for berries/fruit is published on the forest's own pages; all forest-product removal (including special/non-wood forest products) requires a permit. Collection is entirely prohibited in Research Natural Areas, Special Interest Areas, Wilderness/recommended Wilderness, developed recreat
forests.mushNote: Mushrooms are listed among the forest's Non-Wood Forest Products examples and require a permit to collect (the forest issues a free-use permit for personal mushroom gathering). Same permit prohibition applies as for other forest products.

forests.foodLimit: Delta National Forest publishes no confirmable personal-use rule for gathering edible fruits, nuts, or berries. The National Forests in Mississippi permits page covers only special-use permits and does not address food gathering. Contact the Delta Ranger District in Rolling Fork before harvesting. Occurrence on the map is not permission.
forests.mushNote: No confirmable wild-mushroom rule is published for Delta National Forest. The National Forests in Mississippi permits page does not address mushroom gathering. Contact the Delta Ranger District in Rolling Fork before collecting. A mushroom on the map is not permission to harvest.

forests.foodLimit: The Dixie publishes no confirmable edible-plant gathering rule. Its permits page covers firewood, posts and poles, Christmas trees, and transplants only, and issues other forest product permits case by case at forest offices. Contact a Dixie ranger district before harvesting. Occurrence on the map is not permission.
forests.mushNote: The Dixie publishes no confirmable mushroom gathering rule. Its permits page names only wood products and issues other permits case by case at forest offices. Treat mushroom collecting as unverified, contact a Dixie ranger district first, and remember occurrence on the map is not permission.

forests.foodLimit: No permit and no quantity threshold stated for personal consumption. The "When DON'T You Need A Permit?" guidance frames it as gathering edible material "for your own consumption," i.e., personal (not commercial) use. No numeric limit is published on this page.
forests.mushNote: Mushrooms are the explicit named example: the forest lists "gathering edible material (morels, etc.) for your own consumption" as a case where NO permit is needed. Morels (a mushroom) are the only specific example given, so personal-use mushroom foraging is clearly permit-free here. (Commercial collection would require

forests.foodLimit: Juniper berries (the only edible fruit/berry the permit page itemizes for personal use): 20 gallons/person/day, no permit required (verbatim: "20 gallons/person/day; no permit required."). "No berry collecting will be allowed during closures due to resource protection." Commercial juniper berry collection is $0.45/lb w

forests.foodLimit: No quantity threshold stated; collection must be for personal "own consumption." No forest-specific fruit/berry/nut quantity limit found on the Florida permits page.
forests.mushNote: Mushrooms fall under the same "edible material (morels, etc.) for your own consumption" free-use language — the page's only edible example given is morels (a mushroom). No separate mushroom permit or quantity threshold is stated for the Florida forests, unlike some Western forests.

forests.foodLimit: This forest publishes no confirmable personal-use rule for edible fruits, nuts, or berries, and its live permits page lists only firewood, special-use, and geocache items. Contact an Ozark-St. Francis ranger district before gathering, and note that ginseng harvest is banned here. Occurrence on this map is not permission.
forests.mushNote: No confirmable wild-mushroom rule is published for this forest. Whether personal-use gathering needs a permit is unclear, so contact an Ozark-St. Francis ranger district before collecting. A mushroom on this map is not permission to pick it.

forests.states: Colorado (also extends slightly into Wyoming via the joint admin unit)
forests.foodLimit: No permit-free incidental berry/fruit/nut allowance is described on the forest's own page. forests.mushNote: Free personal-use mushroom permit required, available at all six ranger district offices (valid on the Routt districts). Limit: up to 1 gallon per day, maximum 5 gallons per calendar year per household. forests.foodLimit: Tahoe NF's own page lists "berries" and "nuts" among products for which "Permits are required to collect." No free-use threshold or personal-use exemption is stated on the forest's page; it directs collectors to "check with the ranger district office in the area where you wish to collect for specific information." forests.mushNote: Mushrooms are explicitly covered: the page lists "fungi (including mushrooms)" among products requiring a permit. No personal-use free-collection threshold is stated; the forest directs collectors to the local ranger district office for specifics.

forests.foodLimit: The Bridger-Teton, which includes the former Teton National Forest, publishes no confirmable berry or edible-plant gathering rule, and its permits page prices herbs, roots, and bulbs by request. Contact a Bridger-Teton ranger district before gathering plants. Occurrence on the map is not permission.
forests.mushNote: Mushrooms may be gathered for personal use with no permit, up to 1 gallon per person per day. No commercial harvest, and none in wilderness areas.

forests.foodLimit: The Tonto publishes no confirmable rule for gathering berries, fruit, cactus fruit, or nuts for personal use. Its permit pages cover firewood, events, and commercial uses only. Contact a Tonto ranger district before collecting. Occurrence on the map is not permission.
forests.mushNote: The Tonto publishes no confirmable mushroom gathering rule. Its permit pages cover only firewood, events, and commercial uses. Treat mushroom collecting as unverified, contact a Tonto ranger district first, and remember occurrence on the map is not permission.

forests.foodLimit: Recreational/incidental personal use of reasonable amounts (about 1 pound) of renewable resources such as flowers, berries, nuts, cones, and leaves does not require a permit. forests.mushNote: Mushrooms are classified as a non-wood forest product on the UWC NF; per the forest's guidance a (free) permit is required to collect mushrooms, unlike the no-permit recreational-use allowance for berries/nuts. forests.foodLimit: "small amounts ... for personal use" — no specific quantity given. Fruits explicitly named (blackberries, pawpaws, rose hips). A separate $20 plant-collecting permit (valid 1 year, one per person) applies to certain other plant species beyond the free-use edibles.
forests.mushNote: Mushrooms are explicitly listed in the no-permit personal-use category, same as fruits — Wayne does NOT require a mushroom permit for small personal-use amounts. (Note: this is the forest's general personal-use guidance; it does not impose a species whitelist — the foraging map's own mushroom safety rules still govern

forests.foodLimit: Personal use is free; pinon (pine) nuts capped at 1 bushel for personal use without a permit ("No permit is required to gather up to one (1) bushel of pinon nuts for personal use"). Above that / commercial collection would require a permit. No separate berry/fruit threshold stated on the forest's pages.
forests.mushNote: Forest's Non-Wood/Forest Products page lists "commercial mushroom picking" among activities that need a permit; the personal-vs-commercial framing indicates personal-use mushroom collection does not require a permit. forests.foodLimit: Free, no permit needed for small amounts for personal/family use. "For nuts, berries, or plant materials up to one gallon bag would be considered a small amount." forests.mushNote: The Inyo NF Forest Products Permits page does not mention mushrooms at all. forests.foodLimit: Huckleberries (and other edible berries) free for personal use without a permit up to 10 gallons. Verbatim: "Huckleberries - may be gathered in quantities up to 10 gallons for free without a permit."
forests.mushNote: Tiered: free incidental personal use up to 1 gallon/day (max 5 gallons/season) with no permit; 5-20 gallons/season (5 gal/day limit) requires a FREE personal-use permit. Condition: "All mushrooms shall be split in half lengthwise prior to leaving the site." No raking/digging/ground disturbance; cut at ground level.
